benchmark: Incremental memory — is remember() exact, and does its cost track the memory or the corpus?
why_this_axis: Until now OmniFuse's memory was batch-only: folding a confirmed (query -> documents) pair into the index meant rebuilding it. That was listed as an honest limitation in adaptive_memory.json. A retriever that cannot learn without a rebuild cannot learn in a service.

correctness_bar.claim: after k calls to remember(), the index is what a full rebuild with the same feedback would have produced
correctness_bar.how: compare postings key sets, every idf, and every (doc list, weight array) with `==`. Weights are floats: the bar is BIT equality, not isclose. Then compare top-10 rankings on held-out probes.
correctness_bar.why_so_strict: a fast update that scores even slightly differently is not an optimization, it is a bug with a stopwatch — and it would surface as an unexplained benchmark drift weeks later.

the_mechanism.what_is_local: Evidence fields are excluded from document frequency and are not length-normalized. So N never changes, the content df never changes, every content term's IDF is fixed, and the evidence field's avglen is unused (b=0). Remembering a query therefore rewrites the contributions of exactly ONE document.
the_mechanism.the_one_coupling: A term seen ONLY in evidence has content-df 0 and takes its IDF from the evidence df — which grows as documents remember more. That IDF change is global in principle.
the_mechanism.why_it_stays_cheap: Every posting of such a term is evidence-derived, so the documents to fix are exactly the documents that remember it. The blast radius is the memory, not the corpus. Measured: after 100 memories NFCorpus has 15 such terms out of a 23,610-term vocabulary; after 120 memories KRA has 40 out of 71,284.
the_mechanism.exactness: BM25F folds `idf * tfw(k1+1)/(k1+tfw)` into the postings at build. The index keeps the tfw of evidence-only terms alongside their weights, so when their IDF moves the weight is recomputed from the same expression rather than rescaled — which is why the result is bit-identical rather than merely close.
the_mechanism.an_earlier_wrong_claim: The first prototype asserted the update was PURELY local and skipped the evidence-df coupling. Its postings differed from a rebuild in 1,181 terms. The correctness bar caught it; the mechanism above is what survived.

NFCorpus corpus/10 (en, 363 docs, 30 mem.reading: the control. Ten times fewer documents: the rebuild gets 7.9x cheaper, remember() does not move (1.002 -> 1.019 ms). Cost tracks the memory, not the corpus.

does_it_degrade_as_memory_grows.setup: KRA, 215 memories folded in one at a time, mean ms per 50
does_it_degrade_as_memory_grows.reading: flat. A postings insert is a memmove into an array, so the cost is bounded by posting-list length, not by how much has already been remembered.

memory_axis_through_the_incremental_path.setup: the re-query (paraphrase) axis of adaptive_memory.json, but memories are folded in with remember() instead of a rebuild. Scored by synaptic's own metrics.reciprocal_rank.
memory_axis_through_the_incremental_path.reading: real beats the strongest placebo by 32x on covered queries and both placebos stay at noise — the same signature as the batch path, which is what bit-identity guarantees. 107 memories folded in 178 ms.
memory_axis_through_the_incremental_path.note: this harness re-derives the covered/uncovered split from sorted query ids, so its cold baseline differs slightly from adaptive_memory.json's. The comparison inside each run is the point, not the absolute cold number.

cost.index_state_added: for evidence-bearing indexes only: the evidence df of evidence-only terms, their tfw, and the per-field token totals. Nothing is added to a plain BM25/BM25F index.
cost.build_loop: one  per (term, document) posting — short-circuits on a local bool when there are no evidence fields
cost.static_suite: unchanged: finreg single-hop 0.8400 / nDCG 0.8663 / hit 114 / multi-hop 107 / R@10 0.9250, and the 8 public datasets are bit-identical

cold_path_opt_in_KRA_5234_chunks.reading: opting in costs +2.6% build (one tokenize('') per chunk for the empty memory field) and nothing else: identical index size, identical load, identical search, and bit-identical cold rankings. Measured without tracemalloc, which inflates build ~4x.

honest_limitations: Evidence may only grow. There is no `forget()`: removing a memory would require the document's old evidence tokens, which the index does not keep.

The content fields of a remembering document must be the tokens it was built from. `remember()` re-tokenizes them from the stored chunk, so this holds — but a caller who mutates chunk text behind the index will get a stale posting rather than an error.
This makes memory incremental. The index is still read back into RAM: there is no disk-resident queryable backend.
synaptic's consolidation cascade, embedder and reranker remain unmeasured, as in adaptive_memory.json.
